title: Expressing opposition to Central Business District Tolling Program of New York City.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill expresses Congress’s disapproval of a plan to charge drivers a fee to enter parts of Manhattan in New York City. It aims to halt the congestion pricing program.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Drivers who regularly commute into Manhattan below 60th Street.
- Residents and businesses in or near the affected area of Manhattan.

WHAT CHANGES:
- The bill, if enacted, could block federal funding for the tolling program.
- New York City might need to significantly alter its congestion plan. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will protect commuters and businesses from unfair financial burdens.
- Critics say: It interferes with local decisions about managing traffic and raising funds.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in Congress and is currently under consideration by committees.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

title: Reserved for the Minority Leader.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill outlines procedures for designating a temporary Speaker of the House if the Speaker dies, resigns, or is unable to fulfill their duties. It allows the longest-serving member of the majority party to serve temporarily until a new Speaker is elected. 

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The House of Representatives as an institution.
- Members of the majority political party in the House.
- Potential future Speakers of the House.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Establishes a clear process for temporary leadership succession.
- Specifies the longest-serving member fills the role temporarily. 
- Removes ambiguity about who leads during a Speaker vacancy.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This ensures continuity and stability in House operations during unexpected vacancies.
- Critics say: It could concentrate power within one individual without full election by the House.

STATUS: The bill was introduced in the House on January 9, 2024, and is currently under consideration by the Rules Committee.

WHO IS AFFECTED:

WHAT CHANGES:
- Voters would rank candidates in order of preference.
- If no candidate gets a majority, the last-place candidate is eliminated.
- Votes are redistributed based on voter rankings until a majority is reached.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: RCV ensures winners have broader appeal beyond just a plurality of votes.
- Critics say: RCV can be confusing for voters and potentially delay election results.

title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States to provide certain line item veto authority to the President.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes a change to the U.S. Constitution allowing the President to cancel specific parts of laws Congress has already passed. It’s a type of limited veto power beyond simply approving or rejecting an entire bill.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- Members of Congress 
- Federal agencies implementing laws

WHAT CHANGES:
- The President could reject individual items within spending bills.
- This would give the President more control over federal spending. 
- Congress and the President would need to agree on procedures for this veto.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: It would give the president a tool to cut wasteful government spending.
- Critics say: It could upset the balance of power between the branches of government. 

STATUS: This proposed amendment has been introduced in Congress and is under consideration.
status: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: To inform the Senate that a quorum of the House has assembled and of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill simply informs the Senate that the House of Representatives has met and elected its leaders – the Speaker and the Clerk. It's a formal notification to allow business between the two chambers to begin.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The United States Senate 
- Members of the House of Representatives

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formally starts the process for the House & Senate to work together.
- Creates an official record of the House’s organizational meeting. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This is a necessary procedural step to begin legislative work.
- Critics say: It appears there is no significant debate surrounding this bill.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the Senate and is awaiting further action.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Authorizing the Speaker to appoint a committee to notify the President of the assembly of the Congress.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill allows the Speaker of the House to create a small group to formally tell the President when both chambers of Congress have agreed on a bill. It’s about official notification procedures.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- Members of Congress, specifically the Speaker 
- Congressional staff involved in messaging

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formalizes how the President is informed of passed legislation.
- Creates a specific committee for this single purpose. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This clarifies and streamlines the process of informing the President.
- Critics say: The current informal system works well enough; this is unnecessary.

STATUS: It has been proposed in the House but I don’t have information on its current progress beyond that.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Authorizing the Clerk to inform the President of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill allows the House Clerk to officially tell the President who has been elected as Speaker of the House and also informs them of their own election as Clerk. It’s mostly about formal notification procedures.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The President of the United States
- The newly elected Speaker of the House

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formalizes a process for notifying the President.
- Creates a record of these notifications with the President.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This ensures proper communication and record-keeping between branches of government.
- Critics say: It’s a minor procedural change with little practical impact. 

STATUS: The bill is currently in committee, meaning it is being reviewed before potential consideration by the full House.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Reaffirming that the United States is not a party to the Rome Statute and does not recognize the jurisdiction of the International Criminal Court.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill restates U.S. policy against cooperating with the International Criminal Court (ICC) and clarifies it has no authority within our borders. It’s a reaffirmation of a long-held position.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- U.S. citizens and legal residents. 
- Officials in the Executive Branch dealing with international law.
- Individuals potentially subject to ICC investigation, even if not in the US.

WHAT CHANGES:
- It formally codifies existing non-recognition of the ICC's jurisdiction.
- The bill authorizes sanctions against those assisting ICC investigations.
- It directs the President to ensure no U.S. funds support the ICC. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This protects U.S. sovereignty and prevents politically motivated prosecutions of Americans.
- Critics say: It isolates the U.S. from international efforts to address war crimes and human rights abuses. 

STATUS: The bill passed in the House on March 13, 2024, and is now before the Senate.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: Reaffirming the House of Representatives's commitment to ensuring secure elections throughout the United States by recognizing that the presentation of valid photograph identification is a fundamental component of secure elections.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill states that requiring photo ID at polls is important for election security and affirms the House’s support for it.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Voters in states with or considering photo ID laws.
- Election officials administering voter identification requirements.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formally recognizes photo ID as a key part of secure elections. 
- Doesn't *require* any state to adopt photo ID, just supports the idea.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: Photo ID helps prevent voter fraud and increases public confidence in elections.
- Critics say: Such requirements can disproportionately burden marginalized voters who may have difficulty obtaining acceptable IDs. 

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House of Representatives but its current status beyond that is uncertain.
status: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

title: A concurrent resolution authorizing the use of the rotunda of the Capitol for the lying in state of the remains of the late James Earl Carter, Jr., 39th President of the United States.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill allows Jimmy Carter’s body to lie in state in the U.S. Capitol Rotunda after his death. It's a symbolic honor reserved for prominent figures.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The family of James Earl Carter, Jr.
- People who wish to pay their respects publicly. 
- Visitors to the U.S. Capitol building.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Jimmy Carter’s remains will be displayed in the Rotunda.
- Security and logistical arrangements will be made for public viewing.
- A period of national mourning will be formally recognized at the Capitol.

WHAT'S DEBATED: 
- Supporters say: This resolution appropriately honors a former president’s lifetime of service.
- Critics say: I have found no publicly available statements outlining criticisms of this specific resolution. 

STATUS: It was introduced in the House on February 18, 2024 and passed there; it then moved to the Senate on February 29, 2024.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Providing the sense of the House of Representatives that the House should not adjourn until the annual appropriation bills within the jurisdiction of all the subcommittees of the Committee on Appropriations for the current fiscal year are enacted into law.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill expresses a desire for Congress to stay in session until all spending bills for the year are passed and become law. It’s essentially a statement of intent, not a binding rule.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of Congress – they'd be expected to remain in session.
- Federal agencies – their funding relies on these appropriation bills.
- The general public – impacted by government services funded by these bills.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Congress *might* stay in session longer to finish spending bills.
- It signals a preference for completing the budget process fully. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This encourages responsible budgeting and avoids temporary funding gaps.
- Critics say: It could limit Congress’ ability to address other urgent issues. 

STATUS: The bill is currently under consideration in the House of Representatives.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Appropriations.

title: Recognizing the importance of access to comprehensive, high-quality, life-affirming medical care for women of all ages.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill expresses support for access to a wide range of healthcare services for women throughout their lives. It doesn’t create any new programs or funding, but states a policy goal.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Women of all ages seeking medical care.
- Healthcare providers and facilities. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- The bill formally declares support for comprehensive women's healthcare.
- It encourages consideration of women’s health needs in existing programs.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This affirms the importance of ensuring all women have access to necessary medical care.
- Critics say: The phrase "life-affirming" implies a specific viewpoint on reproductive healthcare and is unnecessary. 

STATUS: It has been introduced in [legislative body - source does not specify] and is awaiting further action.

title: Disapproving of the rule submitted by the Department of Homeland Security relating to "Modernizing H-2 Program Requirements, Oversight, and Worker Protections".
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill seeks to overturn a new rule created by the Department of Homeland Security regarding the H-2 visa program for agricultural and non-agricultural workers. It essentially blocks that rule from going into effect.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Farm owners and employers using temporary, seasonal labor.
- Workers who may be eligible for H-2 visas. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- The new DHS rule regarding H-2 visa requirements would not take effect.
- Current H-2 visa program rules would remain in place.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill protects American jobs and ensures a stable workforce for farms.
- Critics say: Blocking the rule weakens worker protections and could lead to exploitation. 

STATUS: It is currently under consideration by Congress, having passed in the House and been received in the Senate on May 9, 2024.
status: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Regarding consent to assemble outside the seat of government.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill would change the rules for getting permission to hold demonstrations or gatherings near state government buildings. It aims to balance First Amendment rights with security concerns.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People wanting to protest or assemble near state capitol grounds.
- State and local law enforcement responsible for permitting events. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Requires permits for assemblies of eight or more people.
- Permits must be applied for at least 14 days in advance.
- The bill clarifies what activities require a permit versus don’t. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will ensure public safety and allow planned events to occur smoothly.
- Critics say: It could unfairly restrict the right to protest by creating hurdles for organizers. 

STATUS: The bill is currently in committee, where it is being discussed and potentially amended.
status: Received in the Senate.

title: Electing officers of the House of Representatives.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill changes how the House of Representatives chooses its leaders – specifically, who becomes Speaker and other key positions. It aims to make those elections happen faster and with a simpler process.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of the House of Representatives
- Potential candidates for leadership roles 
- The public, due to government function

WHAT CHANGES:
- Requires a majority vote for Speaker on the first ballot.
- Allows a motion to vacate the Speaker’s chair after a set period.
- Creates a process for nominating and electing officers quickly. 
- Limits debate during officer elections in the House.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will ensure stability and efficient leadership selection in the House.
- Critics say: It weakens the power of individual representatives to challenge leadership.

STATUS: The bill passed the House on May 10, 2024, and is now before the Senate.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Fair Representation Amendment
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes a change to how congressional districts are drawn, aiming for more competitive elections and fairer representation of voters. It would establish an independent commission to handle redistricting instead of state legislatures.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Residents of all states currently using legislative redistricting.
- Political parties seeking to maintain or gain power. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Redistricting will be handled by independent commissions.
- The goal is to create more competitive congressional districts.
- Commissions would prioritize communities of interest and voting patterns.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill reduces gerrymandering, giving voters more choice and accountability.
- Critics say: It could lead to unintended consequences and diminish local control over representation. 

STATUS: The bill is currently under consideration by relevant committees in Congress.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

title: HEALTH Act
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to improve access to and lower costs for healthcare services, particularly regarding telehealth and preventative care. It also addresses some hospital payment issues.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People who use telehealth for medical appointments.
- Hospitals, especially those in rural areas.
- Individuals with chronic health conditions needing preventative care. 
- Health insurance companies and beneficiaries.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Expands access to Medicare benefits through telehealth services.
- Requires Medicare to cover annual wellness visits without cost-sharing.
- Changes how hospitals are paid for certain outpatient services.
- Increases funding for rural healthcare network grants.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill will make healthcare more affordable and accessible, especially for those in underserved areas.
- Critics say: The changes to hospital payments could negatively impact some facilities’ financial stability. 

STATUS: As of November 26, 2023, the bill has passed the House and is now before the Senate.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

- Members of Congress (House & Senate)
- People interested in the presidential transition process. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formally establishes January 6, 2025 as the count day.
- Reaffirms existing procedures for counting electoral votes. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:

title: Small Business Prosperity Act of 2025
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to help small businesses by increasing a tax deduction they can take for startup costs and making certain business assets eligible for faster depreciation. It intends to encourage investment in small companies.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Small business owners and entrepreneurs.
- People who invest in small businesses. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- The amount deductible for startup/organization costs increases.
- Certain assets can be depreciated more quickly for tax purposes.
- Businesses may have a lower taxable income, potentially reducing taxes paid.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill will stimulate economic growth by encouraging small business investment and job creation. 
- Critics say: The benefits primarily go to businesses that are already established and may not significantly impact overall economic growth.

title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.

WHAT CHANGES:

- It establishes a new process for filling vacancies on committees.

WHAT'S DEBATED:

- Critics say: It concentrates too much power in the hands of party leaders, reducing individual member influence.

title: Rescinding the subpoenas issued by the January 6th Select Committee on September 23, 2021, October 6, 2021, and February 9, 2022, and withdrawing the recommendations finding Stephen K. Bannon, Mark Randall Meadows, Daniel Scavino, Jr., and Peter K. Navarro in contempt of Congress.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to cancel subpoenas issued by the January 6th Select Committee and remove findings that certain individuals were in contempt of Congress. It would effectively undo some actions taken during the committee’s investigation.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Stephen K. Bannon
- Mark Randall Meadows
- Daniel Scavino, Jr.
- Peter K. Navarro 
- The January 6th Select Committee (historically)

WHAT CHANGES:
- Previously issued subpoenas would be officially cancelled.
- Contempt findings against the named individuals would be withdrawn. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill corrects an overreach by the previous committee and ensures fair treatment.
- Critics say: It undermines Congressional authority and accountability for those who refused to cooperate with a legitimate investigation.

STATUS: The bill has been proposed in Congress, but its current progress is uncertain.
status: Referred to the Committee on Rules, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill changes how members are selected for key committees in the House of Representatives, specifically those dealing with important topics like budget and rules. It aims to give more power to individual members rather than just party leaders.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- All Members of the House of Representatives 
- Committee chairs and ranking members
- People interested in how Congress operates

WHAT CHANGES:
- Allows all House members to vote for committee assignments.
- Shifts some power away from party leadership over committees.
- Requires a public record of how each member votes on assignments.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will make committees more representative and accountable to all members, not just leaders.
- Critics say: It could lead to gridlock or empower extreme factions within the House. 

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House of Representatives and is currently awaiting consideration by the relevant committee.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Expressing the sense of the House of Representatives that the Parthenon Marbles should be returned to Greece.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill asks for the return of ancient sculptures – known as the Parthenon Marbles – currently held by museums in the United Kingdom, to their country of origin, Greece. It's a non-binding resolution expressing Congress’s opinion.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The British Museum and other UK institutions holding the marbles.
- The government and people of Greece. 
- Museums and cultural heritage organizations generally.

WHAT CHANGES:
- No immediate legal change; it's a statement of Congressional belief.
- Could pressure for negotiation regarding the sculptures’ ownership.
- May influence US policy towards international cultural property disputes.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: Returning the marbles is a matter of historical and cultural justice for Greece.
- Critics say: The marbles were legally acquired by Britain, and other museums may claim similar rights to artifacts.

STATUS: This resolution has been introduced in the House of Representatives and currently sits with the Committee on Foreign Affairs.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: To repeal the National Voter Registration Act of 1993.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill would get rid of a federal law that makes it easier for people to register to vote, like at motor vehicle agencies. It aims to shift control of voter registration entirely to states.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People who move frequently and rely on federal registration options. 
- State election officials responsible for running registrations.
- Voters using same-day or automatic voter registration programs.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Federal agencies could no longer offer voter registration forms.
- States would have complete control over voter registration rules.
- Existing national voter registration database provisions would be eliminated. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This returns power to the states as intended by the Constitution regarding elections.
- Critics say: It will make it harder for eligible citizens to register and could decrease voter turnout. 

STATUS: The bill has been proposed in Congress but its current status is uncertain.
status: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

title: Improving Science in Chemical Assessments Act
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to require federal agencies to use more modern scientific approaches when evaluating the safety of chemicals. It focuses on replacing older testing methods with those considered more accurate and humane. 

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People exposed to potentially harmful chemicals in everyday products.
- Chemical manufacturers who will need to adapt testing procedures.
- Federal scientists involved in chemical risk assessment.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Agencies must prioritize using non-animal testing methods when available.
- Greater emphasis on real-world exposure data, not just high-dose lab tests. 
- Increased transparency in how chemical safety is determined.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will lead to better protection of public health and reduce animal testing.
- Critics say: Implementing these changes could delay important chemical assessments and increase costs.

STATUS: The bill has passed the House and is currently before the Senate for consideration.
status: Referred to the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.

title: BRIDGE to Congress Resolution
summary:
OVERVIEW: This resolution encourages the House of Representatives to create a non-partisan program – “Building Roads for Democratic Engagement” (BRIDGE) – to help new congressional members connect with each other. It aims to foster relationships across party lines, especially during their first few months in office.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Newly elected Members of Congress.
- Congressional leadership and staff involved in the program. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- New members would participate in relationship-building activities.
- The House could establish a formal BRIDGE orientation program.
- Activities might include facilitated discussions and social events.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will help new members work together more effectively despite political differences. 
- Critics say: It is uncertain if the program would actually change behavior or is simply symbolic.

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the House of Representatives on January 26, 2024 and referred to the Committee on House Administration.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.

title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States extending the right to vote to citizens sixteen years of age or older.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill proposes a change to the U.S. Constitution allowing people who are sixteen and seventeen years old to vote in all elections. It would lower the voting age from eighteen to sixteen.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Citizens aged sixteen and seventeen.
- Election officials and administrators. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Sixteen and seventeen year olds could register to vote.
- States would need to update voter registration processes. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: Lowering the voting age will increase civic engagement among young people.
- Critics say: Sixteen and seventeen year olds may not have sufficient maturity or knowledge to vote responsibly.

STATUS: This proposed amendment has been introduced in Congress, but its future is uncertain as constitutional amendments require broad support.
status: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill changes how members are selected for important, permanent committees in the House of Representatives. It aims to give individual representatives more say in committee assignments.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of the House of Representatives
- Committee chairs and ranking members
- Future applicants seeking committee positions 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Representatives can rank their preferred committees.
- The steering committee must consider those preferences.
- More transparency is required in the selection process. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This will make committees more representative and accountable to all members.
- Critics say: It could lead to less qualified individuals on key committees, prioritizing personal preference over expertise. 

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House of Representatives and is currently with committee(s).
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: A resolution to constitute the majority party's membership on certain committees for the One Hundred Nineteenth Congress, or until their successors are chosen.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill outlines how many seats each political party gets on specific House of Representatives committees. It ensures the party with more members controls these committees.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of the House of Representatives.
- Committee staff working in Congress.

WHAT CHANGES:
- The majority party will have a larger number of committee seats.
- Minority party representation on those committees is defined. 

WHAT'S DEBATED: 
- Supporters say: This reflects the will of voters and ensures efficient legislative work.
- Critics say: It could lead to overly partisan outcomes in committee decisions.

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the House, meaning it’s under consideration but hasn’t passed any votes yet.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S40; text: CR S42-43)

title: A resolution honoring the life and legacy of President Jimmy Carter and commending President Jimmy Carter for his life-long career of public service, humanitarian leadership, diplomacy, and courageous advocacy.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill is a formal expression of respect and recognition for former President Jimmy Carter’s life and work. It praises his contributions to public service and global issues.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The family of Jimmy Carter.
- People interested in presidential history.

WHAT CHANGES:
- There are no concrete changes to laws or policies. 
- This resolution creates an official record of Congress’s esteem for President Carter.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: It is appropriate to honor a dedicated public servant with a lasting tribute.
- Critics say: I am uncertain if there is any significant debate surrounding this resolution. 

STATUS: The resolution has been introduced in the House of Representatives.
status: Submitted in the Senate, considered, and agreed to without amendment and with a preamble by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S76; text: CR S81)

title: To terminate the designation of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan as a major non-NATO ally, and for other purposes.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill would remove a special status given to Pakistan – being designated a “major non-NATO ally” – and require reports on Pakistan’s actions related to terrorism.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- The government of Pakistan.
- U.S. foreign policy & national security officials. 
- People interested in U.S.-Pakistan relations.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Pakistan would no longer have this specific ally designation.
- The President must report on certain Pakistani actions.
- Reports are required regarding support for terrorism and nuclear weapons.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: Removing the status sends a clear message about concerns with Pakistan’s conduct.
- Critics say: This could harm important counterterrorism cooperation with Pakistan.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in Congress and is currently under consideration by committees.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: Stop Imposing Woke Ideology Abroad Act
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to restrict U.S. funding to international organizations that promote what it defines as “woke ideology,” specifically regarding gender and sexual orientation. It seeks to prevent taxpayer dollars from supporting these concepts overseas.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- International organizations receiving U.S. funds.
- Taxpayers who contribute to federal funding. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- Funding could be cut for groups promoting certain ideologies.
- The State Department would need to define “woke ideology.”
- Organizations would have to certify they don't promote these ideas.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill protects taxpayer money from funding harmful or inappropriate agendas abroad.
- Critics say: It unfairly targets LGBTQ+ rights and limits important global health and development work. 

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House of Representatives on February 29, 2024, and referred to committee.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

title: Recognizing the 125th anniversary of organized Okinawan immigration to the United States.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill honors the history and contributions of people from Okinawa who have immigrated to the United States, recognizing 125 years since organized immigration began. 

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- People of Okinawan descent living in the U.S.
- Those interested in Asian American history.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Formally recognizes Okinawan Americans’ heritage.
- Directs flag display on federal buildings for the anniversary. 

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This bill acknowledges a significant part of American immigration history and celebrates Okinawan culture.
- Critics say: I am unable to find any publicly available criticisms of this specific bill.

STATUS: It was introduced in the House on February 29, 2024, and is currently with the Committee on Oversight and Accountability.
status: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

title: Electing Members to certain standing committees of the House of Representatives.
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill changes how members are selected for key, permanent committees in the House of Representatives—specifically, the Ways and Means Committee and the Rules Committee. It shifts some power away from committee chairs and party leaders.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Members of the House of Representatives.
- Committee chairs and party leadership in the House. 

WHAT CHANGES:
- The Steering Committee would have less direct control over appointments.
- More members could directly vote on who serves on these committees.
- The Ways & Means and Rules Committees will have a larger, more diverse membership.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This change will make committee selections fairer and more democratic for all House members. 
- Critics say: It could weaken the ability of leaders to ensure effective committee work and experienced leadership.

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in the House, referred to committee, and is awaiting further action.
status: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

title: Pregnant Women Health and Safety Act of 2025
summary:
OVERVIEW: This bill aims to increase federal protections for pregnant women seeking healthcare, particularly regarding emergency medical care. It clarifies that states cannot prohibit doctors from providing medically necessary abortions in emergency situations.

WHO IS AFFECTED:
- Pregnant individuals needing emergency medical care.
- Doctors and other healthcare providers. 
- States with restrictive abortion laws.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Federal law would supersede conflicting state laws in emergencies.
- Hospitals receiving Medicare funding must provide stabilizing treatment.
- Doctors could face penalties for violating patient protections.

WHAT'S DEBATED:
- Supporters say: This ensures women receive vital care during life-threatening pregnancies.
- Critics say: It infringes on states’ rights and may encourage abortions. 

STATUS: The bill has been introduced in Congress and is currently under consideration by committees.
status: Referred to the Committee on the Judiciary, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned.
